clusters.title: Thousands who visited Sydney dentist urged to get blood test
clusters.neutral_headline: Thousands who visited Sydney dentist urged to get blood test
bullet_summary: Reported by The Age, 9News, ABC News Australia

items.title: Thousands who visited Sydney dentist urged to get blood test
items.standfirst: Thousands of people who visited a Sydney dentist over 25 years have been urged to get tested for several diseases, including HIV, due to the risk of potential exposure.
items.content: Thousands of people who visited a Sydney dentist over 25 years have been urged to get tested for several diseases, including HIV, due to the risk of potential exposure.

items.title: Thousands of patients of Sydney dentist urged to get tested for bloodborne viruses
items.standfirst: An audit at the practice "identified deficiencies in the sterilisation of equipment and in cleaning practices".
items.content: Thousands of people who visited a Sydney dentist over a 25-year period have been urged to get tested for viruses after an audit raised concerns about sterilisation and cleaning at the practice.NSW Health confirmed authorities had investigated the practice of Dr William Tam at Suite B, 2 Albert Street, Strathfield, and uncovered concerns about infection control practices and record-keeping.Sydney Local Health District Staff Specialist Dr Zeina Najjar said an audit occurred in April following a complaint, which found "deficiencies in the sterilisation of equipment and in cleaning practices".READ MORE: What the budget tax reforms mean for rents, housing prices and supply"[The Dental Council of NSW] conducted the initial audit and flagged the issue with [the Sydney Local Health District]. "There have been no cases of bloodborne virus transmission linked to this practice, but we are still recommending testing as a precautionary measure," Najjar said.While Tam is now retired and no longer registered as a dental practitioner, he saw thousands of patients.Najjar said that due to "poor" and "incomplete" record-keeping, NSW Health has no way to contact all of its patients. "As a precaution, we are asking all of Dr Tam's previous patients to seek testing for bloodborne viruses," Clinical Director, Public Health, Sydney Local Health District, Dr Leena Gupta, said.READ MORE: The federal budget in graphs"The poor infection control practices at Dr Tam's practice means all former patients may be at low risk of a blood-borne virus infection, which can have serious and long-lasting health impacts."People with HIV, hepatitis B, or hepatitis C may not have any symptoms for decades, so it is important that people at risk of these infections are tested, so that they can access treatment as appropriate. There are effective treatments available for all three conditions."Patients of Dr Tam have been advised to see their local GP or contact HealthdirectREAD MORE: 'I don't think about Americans' financial situation': Donald TrumpNEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

items.title: Patients of retired Sydney dentist advised to test for bloodborne viruses
items.standfirst: Retired dentist William Tam may have exposed his patients to hepatitis B, hepatitis C and HIV at his clinic, according to NSW Health.
items.content: Retired dentist William Tam may have exposed his patients to hepatitis B, hepatitis C and HIV at his clinic, according to NSW Health.

clusters.title: Alleged plot to blow up Melbourne ATM backfires
clusters.neutral_headline: Alleged plot to blow up Melbourne ATM backfires

items.title: Alleged plot to blow up Melbourne ATM backfires
items.standfirst: A bumbling thief has landed himself in hospital, after he and two accomplices allegedly tried to blow up an ATM at Melbourne's Queen Victoria Market.
items.content: A bumbling thief has landed himself in hospital, after he and two accomplices allegedly tried to blow up an ATM at Melbourne's Queen Victoria Market.

items.title: Man critically injured after plot to blow up ATM backfires
items.standfirst: An alleged would-be thief has suffered life-changing injuries after a homemade bomb was used to blow up an ATM in Melbourne's CBD overnight.
items.content: An alleged would-be thief has suffered life-changing injuries after a homemade bomb was used to blow up an ATM in Melbourne's CBD overnight.Three people used the explosives to break open the ATM at Melbourne's Queen Victoria Market on Victoria Street around 2.45am, police allege."The homemade device ignited in the face of one of the men, leaving him with serious and life-changing injuries," a Victoria Police spokesperson said.READ MORE: Historical Sydney department store revived by great grandsonThe trio fled on foot, before the injured man was found a short time later on Peel Street.He was rushed to hospital under police guard with serious facial injuries, where he remains in a critical condition.Arson and Explosives Squad Detective Inspector Chris Murray appealed for the other offenders to hand themselves in."I have said it before and I will say it again: fire and incendiary devices are volatile, unpredictable and incredibly dangerous in untrained hands," Murray said."Two other men present at the scene fled, leaving their associate severely injured."It is only a matter of time until you are identified and arrested – I would appeal to you to visit your nearest police station and hand yourselves in before then."Police said the incident is not related to a spate of attacks on hospitality venues across Melbourne. The investigation remains ongoing.NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: Football academy praises student who became trapped under train
clusters.neutral_headline: Football academy praises student who became trapped under train

items.title: Football academy praises student who became trapped under train
items.standfirst: An elite Melbourne football academy has thrown its support behind a Melbourne schoolboy who was pulled onto train tracks in a horror accident.
items.content: An elite Melbourne football academy has thrown its support behind a Melbourne schoolboy who was pulled onto train tracks in a horror accident.

items.title: Football academy praises 'much-loved' student who became trapped under train
items.standfirst: An elite Melbourne football academy has thrown its support behind a Year 7 Melbourne schoolboy who was pulled onto train tracks in a horror accident after his bag became snagged.
items.content: An elite Melbourne football academy has thrown its support behind a Year 7 Melbourne schoolboy who was pulled onto train tracks in a horror accident after his bag became snagged.The St Aloysius College student trains with Lustica Football, a high-performance soccer training academy for kids.The academy said in a social media post that 12-year-old Chris was a "much-loved" member. READ MORE: All the winners and losers of the Federal Budget "Chris is currently receiving world-class care and ongoing medical treatment, surrounded by the support of his family and loved ones," Lustica Football said in the post, adding that Chris and his family were in their thoughts and prayers.The young student was in a stable condition at the Royal Children's Hospital on Wednesday morning. Chris was at North Melbourne Station just before 4 pm on Monday afternoon when his bag became caught on the train, causing him to fall onto the track.Firefighters spent more than 30 minutes using hydraulic jacks to lift the train wheels after the boy's leg became trapped underneath it, according to Fire Rescue Victoria."The extrication was lengthy, lasting between 45 minutes to an hour, however we were safely able to get the young person to Royal Children's Hospital," Ambulance Victoria paramedic Alex Hemsley said."Very traumatic scene for all involved. ""A fellow student pressed the platform emergency button and contacted Triple-Zero, while other students communicated clearly with transport authorities," St Aloysius College North Melbourne principal Mary Farah said.Chris was rushed to the Royal Children's Hospital in a critical condition, which has since improved to stable.Counselling is being provided to impacted students, staff and families, the college said.NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: Man assisting police after woman's body found on Perth street
clusters.neutral_headline: Man assisting police after woman's body found on Perth street
bullet_summary: A 35-year-old man is assisting police after a woman's body was found outside of an apartment block in Perth today

items.title: Man assisting police after woman's body found on Perth street
items.standfirst: A 35-year-old man is assisting police after a woman's body was found outside of an apartment block in Perth today.
items.content: A man is in custody and assisting police after a woman was found dead outside an apartment block in Perth today.Police said an unidentified man, believed to be known to the woman, approached paramedics about the body on the Grand Boulevard in Joondalup by paramedics at 9.45am AWST (11.45am AEST).Authorities are investigating the cause of the woman's death and whether there are any suspicious circumstances. READ MORE: Man critically injured after plot to blow up ATM backfires The woman has not yet been formally identified at this stage. Earlier today, WA Police detective inspector Jayd Morawski urged the unidentified man to contact police."We want to provide a full picture, so we know exactly what occurred, so we can provide answers to the family and provide answers to the state coroner," Morawski said."There is a conversation required, there are answers that need to be given and he can provide those answers, so we'd like him to do that on his own volition."Ultimately, we have a lot of resources and a lot of technology that we can deploy to find this person."We know who he is and the best thing he can do is hand himself in to police."In an update later this evening, police said a 35-year-old was in their custody and assisting officers with their investigation.Police do not believe there is any threat to the wider community."This is an isolated matter and as such, we don't think the community is at any risk," Morawski said.Police are also seeking CCTV and dashcam footage from the Grand Boulevard between 9.30am and 9.45am today.NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here
